title: Panchatantra Moral Fables Chapter 4 (Full Canonical Coverage — Book 2 Completion)
revision_note: Batch 4 (2026-07-23): Begins full-canonical-coverage expansion toward Arthur W. Ryder's complete 1925 Panchatantra (82 numbered inserted tales across Books I-V, per the standard count excluding the 5 frame tales). This batch completes Book 2 (The Winning of Friends), which has 9 inserted tales total; 2 (The Bharunda Birds, The Mice That Set Elephants Free) were already ingested in earlier batches, and this file adds the remaining 7: Gold's Gloom, Mother Shandilee's Bargain, Self-Defeating Forethought, Mister Duly, Soft the Weaver, Hang-Ball and Greedy, and Spot's Captivity. All 7 are NEW to the app's reader library (src/lib/katha-library.ts) as well as the AI corpus -- unlike prior batches, which only matched existing reader stories, this batch required writing new Katha entries (English + Shoonaya-curated Hindi paraphrase) from scratch, since no reader entry previously existed for these titles. Source: theoceanofstories.blogspot.com (Laura Gibbs, University of Oklahoma), explicitly attributed to Ryder's 1925 translation, cross-checked against the blog's own 'Panchatantra Index' page (2015/01/panchatantra-index.html) which lists exact story URLs for every Book 1-4 tale -- the same verified source used for Books 1-4 in prior batches. Book 2 (9 inserted tales) is now 100% covered in both the reader and the AI corpus.

content.text:
In the southern country is a city called Maidens' Delight, and in the neighbourhood a shrine to Shiva. In a cell nearby lived a hermit named Crop-Ear. During his begging hour he would fill his alms-bowl with dainties from the city, eatables jellified, melting in the mouth, toothsome, flavoured with sugar, treacle, and pomegranate. Then, returning to his cell, he satisfied himself according to the ordinance, hid what food was left in the alms-bowl, and hung it on a peg, keeping it for the servants' breakfast. On this food I subsisted with my companions.

Since I nibbled his food, however carefully he hid it, the hermit was disgusted, and in fear of me he moved it from place to place, always hanging it higher. Even so I got at it easily enough and ate it.

Now one day a guest arrived, a holy man named Wide-Bottom. And Crop-Ear welcomed him... At night they lay on the same couch and started to relate pious tales. But Crop-Ear's thoughts were so preoccupied with mice that he kept striking the alms-bowl with a frazzled bamboo and returned an absentminded answer to Wide-Bottom as he told a pious tale.

Then the guest grew extremely angry... When he heard this, Crop-Ear was terrified and said: "Do not speak thus, holy sir... There is a villainous mouse that jumps and climbs to my alms-bowl, however high I hang it, and he eats my leavings... the villain has such cleverness in jumping as to put cats, monkeys, and other creatures to the blush."

Then Wide-Bottom said: "But have you found the mouse-hole anywhere?" ... "Surely," said the other, "his hole is over his hoard. Beyond question, the fragrance from his hoard makes him spry." ... "In that case," said the guest, "you and I must wake early, so as to follow their tracks together, while the footprints still dirty the floor."

Now when I heard the villain's speech fall like a thunderbolt, I thought: "Ah, this spells ruin for me..." So I was terrified, deserted the beaten track to my fortress, and with my followers started on another track.

Then a prodigious cat met us, and seeing the whole pack before him, pounced into our midst... Thereupon the holy man, perceiving that the floor was smeared with drops of blood, followed the trail to the fortress, and began to ply the pickaxe. As he dug, he came upon the hoard over which I had lived so long, and the smell of which used to guide me back to the fortress.

Then Wide-Bottom was filled with glee and said: "Now, Crop-ear, sleep in peace. It was the smell of this that enabled the mouse to wake you." So they took the hoard and turned to the cell.

Now when I returned to the spot, I could not bear to look at the sad, disturbing sight... Still, when the sun had laid his thousand beams to rest, I went with my companions to the same cell... Then I made a desperate jump for the alms-bowl, but missed and fell to the floor.

When I heard this, I reflected: "Alas! It is true, though it is my enemy who says it. For today I have not the power to jump a mere finger's breadth. A curse upon a fellow's life without money!"

With this broken-spirited lamentation I saw my own hoard of wealth converted into a pillow for my enemy, and at dawn I crept into my fortress - a failure.

After reaching this conclusion [to recover the treasure], I went there at night and gnawed a hole in the bag after he had gone to sleep. Thereupon that dreadful holy man awoke and struck me on the head with the frazzled bamboo. Yet somehow I escaped death - predestination, you see.

And Gold continued: "After these reflections, I recovered from my money-madness. For there is much wisdom in this: Not rank, but character, is birth; it is not eyes, but wits, that see; true learning it's, to cease from wrong; contentment is prosperity."
content.curated_lesson: A mouse named Gold lived beneath a hermit's cell, feasting nightly on the leftovers the hermit hid away. However high the hermit hung his bowl, Gold leaped up to it with ease — a strength that amazed everyone who saw it. One night a guest of the hermit noticed this and reasoned aloud: a creature does not gain such daring for no reason — he must be living near a hidden treasure, for the nearness of wealth alone can make the timid bold. The guest and the hermit traced Gold's trail to his burrow and dug there, uncovering the very hoard of gold that Gold had been living above for years without ever knowing it consciously — only feeling its strange power. The moment the gold was carried away, Gold tried his usual leap and fell flat on the floor. His own followers mocked him and deserted him, for they too had only respected the strength his nearness to wealth had given him. Ashamed and powerless, Gold reflected through the night: it was never courage that had made him bold, only the comfort of gold nearby — and a creature who measures his own worth by his possessions will always be at the mercy of losing them. In time Gold made peace with this truth. He stopped chasing the smell of treasure and found that real steadiness — the kind no one can dig up and carry away — comes only from within.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
At one time I asked a certain Brahman in a certain town for shelter during the rainy season, and this he gave me. So there I lived, occupied with pious duties.

One day I woke betimes, and listening to a conversation between my host and his wife, I heard the Brahman say: "My dear, tomorrow will be the winter solstice, an extremely profitable season. So I will go to another village in search of donations. And you, in honour of the sun, should give some Brahman food to the extent of your ability."

But his wife snapped at him harshly, saying: "Who would give food to a poor Brahman like you? ..." At this the Brahman was terrified and he stammered reassurances about the value of giving even a little.

After this preachment, the wife said: "Well, I believe I have a bit of sesame grain in the house. I will grind it into flour and feed a Brahman." And her husband, having received her promise, went off to another village.

Then the wife softened the sesame grains in hot water, hulled them, placed them in the hot sun, and returned to her chores in the house. In this state of affairs a dog made water in the dish of grain, and she thought when she saw it: "Dear me! See how shrewd fate is... Well, I will take them to some neighbour's house, and make an exchange, unhulled for hulled. For anybody will bargain on those terms."

So she put her grain in a basket and went from house to house, saying: "Who cares to exchange sesame unhulled for sesame hulled?"

Now she happened to enter with her grain a house which I had entered to beg alms, and she made her offer there. The housewife was delighted and took the hulled grain in exchange for unhulled.

Later, her husband came home and asked: "My dear, what does this mean?" And she told him: "I made a bargain, hulled sesame for unhulled." Over this he pondered, then said: "To whom did this grain belong?" And his son Kamandaki told him: "To Mother Shandilee."

Then he said: "My dear wife, she is mighty shrewd at a bargain. You had better throw this sesame away. 'It's certain Mother Shandilee, If bargaining in sesame - Her hulled grains for the unhulled kind - Has some good reason in her mind.'"
content.curated_lesson: A poor Brahman's wife had set out sesame grain to dry in the sun after hulling it with great effort, meaning to feed a guest that evening as her husband had asked. While she was occupied elsewhere, a stray dog fouled the grain. She could not bear to throw away what had cost her so much labor, so she thought of a plan: she would offer to trade her hulled grain, unhulled portion for portion, with any neighbor willing to exchange. She went from door to door calling her offer, and a housewife nearby — glad to save the work of hulling — gladly traded her own raw, unhulled sesame for Shandilee's hulled grain, unaware of what had happened to it. When Shandilee's husband later learned of the trade, he did not scold her for the small deception. Instead he grew thoughtful and said only this: whenever Mother Shandilee strikes a bargain that seems to favor her a little too easily, you may be certain she has good reason in her mind. The saying spread beyond that household, becoming a gentle warning: a bargain that looks effortlessly generous is rarely without a hidden story — it pays to wonder why the other side is so willing.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
There was once a hillman in a certain place who set out to increase his sins by hunting. As he walked along, he met a boar that resembled the top of Sooty Mountain. Straightway he drew an arrow as far as his ear... Then with a sharp arrow he shot the boar, who in turn angrily tore the hillman's stomach with a pointed fang that shone like the crescent moon, so that the man fell dead. The boar also, after killing the hunter, died in torment from the arrow-wound.

At this point a starving jackal reached the spot in his aimless wanderings. When he spied a boar and a hunter, both dead, he gleefully thought: "Fate is kind to me, providing this unlooked-for store of food... Now I will eat in such a way as to have sustenance for many days. I will begin with the sinew wrapped round the bow-tip. I will hold it in my paws and eat very slowly. For the saying goes: Consumption of a treasure earned should very slowly follow, as wise men sip elixir down, not bolt it at a swallow."

After these reflections, he took into his mouth the sinew with its end hanging from the bow. And when the gut snapped, the bow-tip pierced the roof of his mouth and came out like a topknot. And the jackal perished from the pain of it.

"And that is why I say: Indulge in no excessive greed (A little helps in time of need) But one, by greed excessive led, Perceived a topknot on his head."
content.curated_lesson: A hunter shot a great boar, but the wounded boar gored him fatally with his tusk before dying himself from the arrow. Both lay dead in the forest when a starving jackal wandered by and found this unexpected feast. Delighted at his fortune, the jackal resolved to make the food last many days rather than gorge himself at once. He decided to begin with the sinew of the hunter's bow, nibbling it slowly and carefully, the way a wise man sips a rare elixir rather than gulping it down. As he gnawed through the taut sinew at the bow's tip, the string suddenly snapped. The released bow-tip sprang up and pierced clean through the roof of his mouth. The jackal died instantly from the very carefulness that was meant to preserve his fortune — his forethought about rationing had blinded him to the danger hidden in the very first bite. The tale is remembered as a warning about greed dressed up as prudence: planning carefully to hoard more than you need can be more dangerous than simple hunger.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain city lived a merchant named Ocean. His son picked up a book at a sale for a hundred rupees. In this book was the line: What's duly his, a man receives.

Now Ocean saw it and asked his son: "My boy, what did you give for this book?" "A hundred rupees," said the son. "Simpleton!" said Ocean, "if you pay a hundred rupees for a book with one line of poetry written in it, how do you calculate to make money? From this day you are not at home in my house." After this wigging, he showed him the door.

This melancholy rebuff drove the young man to another country far away... Now a native asked him: "Where are you from, sir? What might your name be?" And he replied: "What's duly his, a man receives." ... This is how he came by his nickname of Mister Duly.

Now a princess named Moonlight... was smitten by the arrows of Love [for a passing prince], and said to her friend: "Dear girl, you must make an effort to bring us together this very day." ... But when night came, the prince thought it over [and refused to go, judging it dishonourable]. So after full reflection he did not go to her.

But Mister Duly was roaming through the night and spied a strap hanging down the wall of a fine stucco house. Out of curiosity mingled with bravado he took hold and climbed. Now the princess, being perfectly confident that he was the right man, treated him with high consideration... Then she went to bed with him... But she said: "...Why don't you realize this and talk to me?" And he replied: "What's duly his, a man receives." When she heard this, her heart stopped beating, and she sent him down the strap in a hurry.

So he made for a tumble-down temple and went to sleep. Presently a policeman... found him asleep... said: "This temple is deserted. Go and sleep in my bed." And he agreed, but made a blunder, lying down in the wrong bed [that of the policeman's daughter, Naughty, who mistook him for her own secret lover and married him by the ceremony used in heaven]... But she said: "Even yet you do not talk nicely with me. Why not?" And he replied: "What's duly his, a man receives." ... So she gave him a sorrowful scolding and sent him packing.

As he walked along a business street, there approached a bridegroom named Fine-Fame... At this moment an elephant reached the spot, running amuck... the crowd was in a hubbub... In this crisis Mister Duly perceived the girl [the bride], all alone, her eyes dancing with terror, and with the words: "Don't worry. I will save you," manfully reassured her, put his right arm around her, and with enormous sang-froid gave the elephant a cruel scolding. And the elephant - it was fate's doing - actually went away.

When the story got abroad... the princess... the policeman's daughter... and the king in turn... arrived at the spot... "Speak without apprehension. What sort of business is this?" And Mister Duly said: "What's duly his, a man receives." Then the princess remembered, and she said: "This law not even God can break." Then the policeman's daughter said: "My heart is not surprised, nor grieves." And the merchant's daughter said: "For what is mine, no strangers take."

Then the king promised immunity to one and all, arrived at the truth by piecing their narratives together, and ended by respectfully giving Mister Duly his own daughter, together with a thousand villages. Then he bethought himself that he had no son, so he anointed Mister Duly crown prince.
content.curated_lesson: A merchant's son once paid a hundred gold coins for an old book that contained only a single line: "What is duly his, a man receives." His furious father disowned him for such foolishness and threw him out of the house. Wandering to a distant city, the young man became known only by that line, since he answered every question — his name, his home, his purpose — with the same words. People began calling him Mister Duly. A chain of strange coincidences followed him: he wandered by mistake into a princess's chamber, then a policeman's house, then found himself in the wrong bed entirely, sleeping instead with a policeman's daughter who had mistaken him for her secret lover. Each time, when asked to explain himself, he simply repeated his one line — and each time, the person he'd confused sent him on his way, bewildered but unharmed. At last, amid a runaway elephant's rampage through a wedding procession, Mister Duly alone stood his ground and calmly saved the terrified bride, driving the beast away while everyone else fled. When the king himself arrived to sort out the extraordinary tangle of events, each woman he had encountered spoke up in turn, each finishing a line of the same old verse, until the truth emerged plainly. The king gave Mister Duly his daughter's hand and, having no son of his own, made him heir to the kingdom. Through every twist — poverty, confusion, danger — nothing was ever truly lost or truly gained that was not already his by destiny.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain town lived a weaver. His name was Soft, and he spent his time making garments dyed in various patterns, fit for such people as princes. But for all his labours, he could not collect a bit of money beyond food and clothes. Yet he saw other weavers, who made coarse fabrics, rolling in wealth, and he said to his wife: "...This city does not offer me a decent living. I am going to move." ... So he went to Growing City, stayed three years, and started home with savings of three hundred gold-pieces.

In mid-journey... he climbed upon a stout branch of a banyan tree and dozed. In the middle of the night... he heard two human figures... abusing each other. The first of them was saying: "Come now, Doer! ...you have, in every possible way, prevented this fellow Soft from getting any capital beyond food and clothes... Why did you give him three hundred gold pieces?" "Now, Deed!" said the other, "I am constrained to give the enterprising a reward in proportion to their enterprise. The final consequence is your affair. Take it from him yourself." On hearing this, Soft awoke and looked for his bag of gold [and found it empty].

So he made up his mind to return to Growing City. There he earned five hundred gold-pieces in just one single year, and started home again by a different road. When the sun went down, he came upon the very same banyan tree... he dozed off on a branch, and saw the same two figures [with the same argument, and] found [his bag] empty.

This plunged him into the depths of gloom, and he thought: "...I will just hang myself from this banyan tree and say goodbye to life." ...when one of the figures appeared in the sky and said: "Do not be so rash, Friend Soft. I am the person who takes your money... ask your heart's desire."

"In that case," said Soft, "give me plenty of money." "My good fellow," said the other, "what will you do with money which you cannot enjoy or give away? For you are to have no use of it beyond food and clothes." But Soft replied: "Even if I get no use of it, still I want it..."

"If things stand so," said the figure, "go once more to Growing City. There dwell two sons of merchants; their names are Penny-Hide and Penny-Fling. When you have observed their conduct, you may ask for yourself the nature of one or the other." With this he vanished.

[Soft visited Penny-Hide, a miser, who could not even feed him due to illness and expense; and then Penny-Fling, a generous spender, who fed and housed him well and was rewarded the very next morning with a gift of money from the king.]

When he saw this, Soft thought: "This Penny-Fling person, even without any capital, is a better kind of thing than that scaly old Penny-Hide. The proverb is right: The Scriptures' fruit is pious homes; Right conduct, that of learnèd tomes; Wives fructify in joy and son; And money's fruit is gifts and fun. So may the blessèd Lord of All make me a person whose money goes in gifts and fun. I see no good in Penny-Hiding." So the Lord of All took him at his word, making him that kind of person.
content.curated_lesson: A weaver named Soft worked hard making fine garments, yet earned barely enough for food and clothes, while coarser weavers around him grew wealthy. Certain that a change of city would change his fortune, he set off for another land despite his wife's doubts about whether effort could really outrun destiny. After three years he started home with three hundred gold coins, but slept one night beneath a banyan tree and overheard two spirits arguing in his dream — one called Doer, one called Deed. Deed insisted that Soft was fated to have nothing beyond food and clothes; Doer replied that he was only bound to reward effort, and the outcome afterward was Deed's affair. Soft woke to find his gold bag empty. Undiscouraged, he earned five hundred gold coins the following year and took a different road home, only to find the very same tree, hear the very same argument, and wake to the very same empty bag. In despair, he prepared to end his life beneath that tree — until one of the spirits appeared and stopped him, revealing himself as the very Deed who took the gold, and offering Soft one wish in place of his lost fortune. Soft asked to understand wealth itself, and the spirit sent him to observe two merchants in the next city: one who hoarded everything fearfully and grew sick and friendless, and another who spent freely on gifts and hospitality and was rewarded by fortune again and again. Watching this, Soft understood what he truly wanted — not a pile of gold he could never touch, but the freedom to give. He asked to become the kind of person whose money flows outward in generosity rather than locked away, and from that day his life found its true wealth.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain town lived a bull named Hang-Ball. From excess of male vigour he abandoned the herd, tore the river-banks with his horns, browsed at will on emerald-tipped grasses, and went wild in the forest.

In that forest lived a jackal named Greedy. One day he sprawled at ease with his wife on a sandy riverbank. At that moment the bull Hang-Ball came down to the same stretch of sand for a drink.

And the she-jackal said to her husband when she saw the hanging testicles: "Look, my dear! See how two lumps of flesh hang from that bull. They will fall in a moment, or a few hours at most. So you must follow him, please."

"My dear," said the jackal, "nobody knows. Perhaps they will fall some day, perhaps not. Why send me on a fool's errand? I would rather stay here with you and eat the mice that come to water..."

"Come," said she, "you are a coward, satisfied with any little thing... Besides, I am dreadfully tired of mouse-flesh, and these two lumps of meat are plainly on the point of falling. You must not refuse me."

So when he had listened to this, he left the spot where mice were to be caught and followed Hang-Ball... So he spent much time wandering with his wife after the bull. But they did not fall. At last in the fifteenth year, in utter gloom he said to his wife: "Loose they are, yet tight; Fall, or stick, my dear? I have watched them now Till the fifteenth year. Let us draw the conclusion that they will not fall in the future either, and return to the old mouse-trail."
content.curated_lesson: A powerful wild bull named Hang-Ball roamed the forest freely, and one day a jackal named Greedy and his wife noticed odd loose folds of flesh swinging beneath him as he walked to the river to drink. The wife jackal was certain these folds would fall off any day, and urged her husband to abandon their comfortable spot — where mice came reliably to drink — and follow the bull instead, so as not to miss the coming feast. Greedy doubted whether "someday" was worth giving up a sure thing today, but his wife pressed him with proverbs about boldness and effort, and reluctantly he agreed to follow the bull, telling himself the reward was surely close at hand. Season after season, year after year, the couple trailed the bull, watching those same folds sway and never fall, growing thinner themselves as they lived on scraps instead of their old reliable mouse-trail. In the fifteenth year, exhausted and no closer to their hoped-for feast, Greedy finally turned to his wife: whether these folds were loose or tight, falling or staying, they had watched them fifteen years for nothing — and it was time to go back to what had once fed them reliably.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
Long ago, when I was six months old, I used to gambol in front of all the rest, as a youngster does... Now we deer have two gaits, called the Jump-Up and the Straightaway. Of these I knew the Straightaway, but not the Jump-Up.

While amusing myself one day, I lost touch with the herd... Now they had avoided a snare by means of the Jump-Up; they stood in a body ahead of me, and waited, all looking at me. But I, ignorant of the Jump-Up, was caught in the hunter's snare.

When the hunter came up, he did not put me to death, for pity softened his heart at the thought: "He is a fawn, fit only for a pet." Instead, he carefully took me home and gave me as a plaything to a prince, who showed his delight at seeing me by giving the hunter a generous reward.

The prince treated me kindly, providing ointments, massage, baths, food, perfumes, and salves... Finally, one day in the rainy season, as the prince reclined on a couch, I observed the lightning, listened to the thunder, and, my heart wistful for my fondly remembered herd, I recited: "When shall I follow on the herd Of coursing deer again? When brace myself against the wind That whistles by? Ah, when?"

"Who said that?" cried the prince, and looked about him, terrified. When he saw me, he thought: "No man said it, but a deer. It is a prodigy. I am undone," and like one possessed by a devil, he tottered from the house... Thinking himself ridden by a demon, he tempted the sorcerers and magicians with a great reward.

Meanwhile, overhasty individuals were striking me with sticks, bricks, and cudgels, but... I was rescued by a certain holy man who said: "Why kill the poor beast?" ...he respectfully said to the prince: "Dear sir, in the rainy season he wistfully remembers his native herd, and therefore recited [the verse]."

On hearing this, the prince was cured of his feverish malady, returned to his normal state, and said to his men: "Douse the poor deer's head in plenty of water, and set him free in the forest he came from." And they did so.
content.curated_lesson: A young fawn named Spot, only six months old and still not skilled at the herd's special escape-leap that older deer used to clear hunters' snares, wandered too far ahead one day and was caught in a trap while the rest of the herd escaped to safety. The hunter, moved by pity at the sight of so young a creature, spared his life and gave him as a gift to a prince, who kept Spot as a beloved pet — bathed, perfumed, fed delicacies, and passed fondly from hand to hand among the curious court. Despite every comfort, Spot never stopped missing the wild herd he had lost. One night in the rainy season, hearing thunder and watching lightning, his heart grew so full of longing that he recited a verse aloud, asking when he would run with the herd again, bracing against the wind. The prince, terrified to hear a voice from nowhere and thinking himself possessed by a demon, fled the room in disarray and offered a great reward to any healer who could cure him. A wise man who happened to be present understood at once and gently explained: it was only the deer, aching with homesickness in the rainy season, that had spoken — not a demon at all. Relieved and moved, the prince ordered Spot released back into the very forest he had come from.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
